tests.name: Markdown structure
tests.raw_response:
## Introduction to Markdown
Markdown is a lightweight markup language that allows you to format text using plain text syntax. It is widely used for writing documentation, README files, and blog posts because it is easy to read and write.

## Key Features
Markdown supports several formatting options that enhance readability:
- **Bold** and *italic* text for emphasis
- Headers of various levels to structure content
- Lists, both ordered and unordered, for organizing items
- Links and images to embed external resources

```python
def is_palindrome(s):
    cleaned = ''.join(c.lower() for c in s if c.isalnum())
    return cleaned == cleaned[::-1]
```

Dear Team,

Please be advised that the Quarterly Planning Meeting, originally scheduled for Thursday, April 10, at 2:00 PM, has been rescheduled. The new meeting time is Monday, April 14, at 10:00 AM in Conference Room B.

The meeting agenda remains unchanged, and we will proceed with the same discussion topics as planned. We sincerely apologize for any inconvenience this change may cause to your schedules and appreciate your flexibility in accommodating this adjustment.

Thank you for your continued dedication and cooperation as we prepare for this important planning session.

Best regards,

[Your Name]
[Your Job Title]

tests.raw_response:
Imagine you are a frequent customer at your favorite coffee shop. Every time you walk in, you order your usual latte. Without a system in place, the barista would have to fetch fresh beans, grind them, measure the milk, and steam it from scratch every single time. This takes time and effort, even though you know exactly what you want.

HTTP caching works like a "frequent customer" shortcut for websites. When you visit a webpage, your computer or a nearby server can save a copy of the images, text, and layout. The next time you visit that same page, instead of traveling all the way back to the main website server to request everything again, your computer simply checks its own saved copy. If the page hasn't changed recently, it shows you the saved version instantly.

This process is like the coffee shop keeping a pre-made cup of your latte ready in a warmer. When you walk in, the barista hands it to you immediately rather than starting the whole process over. This makes the website load much faster for you, saves time, and reduces the work the main server has to do.

However, just as a pre-made coffee might go stale if you wait too long, cached pages have an expiration date. If the website updates its content, the old copy is thrown away, and a fresh version is fetched. This balance ensures you always see the latest information while still enjoying the speed of a quick, cached delivery.

tests.raw_response:
Source C provides the most directly applicable evidence for a startup deciding between remote-first and hybrid policies, as it explicitly compares both models against a third option: the hybrid approach. While Source A advocates for remote work based on individual productivity gains [A] and Source B emphasizes the irreplaceable value of in-office collaboration for culture and innovation [B], Source C synthesizes these competing priorities.

Source C recommends a hybrid model, typically involving two to three days in the office, as the optimal solution. A 2024 randomized controlled trial found that hybrid arrangements matched the productivity of fully remote work while significantly outperforming in-office-only schedules in collaboration quality, innovation, and job satisfaction [C]. Crucially, the hybrid group experienced a 35% reduction in voluntary attrition compared to the in-office control [C]. The source advises that startups should structure in-office days specifically for collaborative activities like brainstorming and onboarding, while reserving remote days for deep individual work. Furthermore, it stresses that for these benefits to materialize, team members must be in the office on overlapping days rather than staggered schedules [C].

tests.raw_response:
Evidence suggests remote work boosts individual productivity but risks long-term collaboration. Source [A] indicates remote knowledge workers produce 5–13.5% more output due to fewer distractions and reduced commute fatigue, while also lowering attrition. However, Source [B] argues these metrics overlook collective value; remote work fragments team networks, slows innovation, and hinders junior mentorship, potentially eroding productivity as social capital depletes.

Consequently, Source [C] proposes hybrid models as the optimal solution. A 2024 study found hybrid schedules (2–3 days in-office) matched remote productivity while significantly improving collaboration, innovation, and retention compared to fully in-office groups. Research confirms deep individual work thrives remotely, whereas brainstorming and onboarding benefit from in-person presence. Crucially, hybrid success depends on coordinating team schedules to ensure overlap. Ultimately, the data supports a balanced approach: leveraging remote work for individual efficiency while maintaining in-person interaction to sustain team cohesion and long-term organizational health.
